The FARs.clue: This FAA regulation sets the performance rules that a certificated repair station must follow.
The FARs.explanation: Part 145 specifically governs certificated repair stations — their certification, personnel, facilities, and operational standards. Part 43 covers how maintenance is performed by anyone authorized; Part 91 covers aircraft operating rules; Part 65 covers airmen certificates. A common mistake is choosing Part 43, but Part 43 governs the work itself, not the repair station organization.
The FARs.learningObjective: Identify the repair station performance regulation

The FARs.clue: These two regulations authorize appropriately rated repair stations to perform pitot-static and transponder inspections.
The FARs.explanation: 91.411 covers altimeter/pitot-static system testing and 91.413 covers transponder testing. Both authorize appropriately rated repair stations. 91.207 covers ELTs. 91.171/177 cover VOR checks and minimum altitudes.
The FARs.learningObjective: State which regulations authorize repair stations for pitot-static and transponder inspections

The FARs.clue: The FAA definition of 'maintenance' includes inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement — but explicitly excludes this.
The FARs.explanation: The FAA defines maintenance as inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement. Preventive maintenance is a separate regulatory category limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate — explicitly excluded from the definition of maintenance.
The FARs.learningObjective: State the FAA definition of maintenance

The FARs.clue: This regulation governs performance rules for aircraft maintenance, preventive maintenance, and alterations — applying to any authorized person, not just repair stations.
The FARs.explanation: Part 43 sets the standards for how maintenance work must be performed and applies broadly — to individual mechanics, repair stations, and other authorized persons. Part 145 governs the repair station organization. Part 43 governs the work itself.
The FARs.learningObjective: State that 14 CFR Part 43 governs maintenance performance rules

The FARs.clue: Per 14 CFR 91.413, transponder systems must be tested and inspected at this interval.
The FARs.explanation: 91.413 requires transponder testing every 24 calendar months — not 12 months or flight hours. The interval is calendar-based, running from the date of the last check regardless of hours flown.
The FARs.learningObjective: State that 91.413 requires transponder testing every 24 calendar months

The FARs.clue: This is the specific regulation number requiring periodic testing and inspection of transponder systems.
The FARs.explanation: 91.413 is specifically for transponders. 91.411 covers altimeter and pitot-static systems. 91.207 covers ELTs. Part 43 covers maintenance standards generally.
The FARs.learningObjective: State the specific regulation requiring periodic transponder testing

The FARs.clue: Under 14 CFR Part 43, this type of maintenance is limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate, for simple listed tasks only.
The FARs.explanation: Preventive maintenance is the only maintenance a certificated pilot (not a mechanic) may legally perform, and only on aircraft they own or operate — limited to simple tasks listed in Part 43 Appendix A.
The FARs.learningObjective: Explain that preventive maintenance under Part 43 is limited to certificated pilots on aircraft they own or operate

Records & Logbooks.clue: Per 14 CFR §43.9, this element is NOT required in a maintenance record entry.
Records & Logbooks.explanation: §43.9 requires: description of work, date, name, certificate number, and return-to-service signature. The aircraft registration number is not a §43.9 required element — the five required elements describe who did the work and what was done, not the aircraft identifier.
Records & Logbooks.learningObjective: State required elements of a maintenance record per §43.9

Records & Logbooks.clue: Of these log entry elements, this one is required by §43.9 but is most often omitted by new technicians.
Records & Logbooks.explanation: The technician's certificate number is most frequently omitted — new techs include their name and signature but forget the certificate number, which §43.9 explicitly requires and which ties the entry to a specific FAA-certificated individual.
Records & Logbooks.learningObjective: List required elements of a maintenance log entry for an avionics repair

Records & Logbooks.clue: Per 14 CFR Part 43, this information is required in a transponder test record but is most often omitted by technicians.
choices: The repair station's work order number
Records & Logbooks.explanation: Part 43 requires the specific test equipment used to be documented — allowing verification that calibrated, appropriate equipment was actually used. Aircraft owner signature, flight hours, and work order numbers are not required transponder test record elements.
Records & Logbooks.learningObjective: Document altimeter and transponder test results per Part 43

Records & Logbooks.clue: After completing a 91.411 pitot-static test, the maintenance record must include the specific test equipment used — including this detail about each instrument, which proves the measurements were valid.
choices: The equipment's original purchase date and vendor
The calibration due date, confirming the equipment was in calibration at the time of the test
The equipment's serial number and firmware version
The equipment manufacturer's ISO 9001 certification number
Records & Logbooks.explanation: Documenting that test equipment was within its calibration interval at the time of use proves the measurements are traceable to known standards.

Records & Logbooks.clue: A technician signs off an avionics installation in the aircraft logbook but omits this specific §43.9 element — which is the single most common regulatory deficiency found during FSDO surveillance of Part 145 shops.
Records & Logbooks.explanation: The technician's certificate number is the most commonly omitted §43.9 element.

Records & Logbooks.clue: A technician discovers that a previous maintenance record entry references the wrong part number for an installed component. Under Part 43, the correct action is this — not simply crossing it out or adding a note.
choices: Make a new corrective entry referencing the original entry, with the correct part number and a new signature
White out the incorrect number and write the correct one with initials and date
Contact the FAA FSDO to request a formal records amendment
Remove the page from the logbook and replace it with a corrected version
Records & Logbooks.explanation: Part 43 maintenance records cannot be erased, whited out, or removed. The correct procedure is a new separate entry referencing the original.

Forms & Approvals.clue: Before performing safety-sensitive functions at a repair station with an FAA-approved drug program, a new hire must pass this.
Forms & Approvals.explanation: FAA regulations require passing a pre-employment drug test before performing safety-sensitive maintenance functions when the station has an FAA-approved drug testing program. Background checks, practical exams, and probationary reviews are not the regulated requirement.
Forms & Approvals.learningObjective: State the pre-employment drug test requirement for safety-sensitive functions

Forms & Approvals.clue: This FAA approval permits modifying a type-certificated aircraft in a way not covered by the original type certificate.
Forms & Approvals.explanation: An STC is the FAA approval for modifications beyond the original type certificate. An AD mandates a safety change. Form 337 documents a major repair or alteration. A PMA approves a replacement part. Only an STC authorizes the modification itself.
Forms & Approvals.learningObjective: State the definition of an STC

Forms & Approvals.clue: This FAA document certifies airworthiness approval and is used to trace the origin of an aircraft part or component.
Forms & Approvals.explanation: Form 8130-3 is the Airworthiness Approval Tag. Form 337 documents major repairs and alterations. Form 8110-3 is a Statement of Compliance for TSO articles. Form 8060-4 is an Airman Certificate.
Forms & Approvals.learningObjective: Explain the purpose of FAA Form 8130-3

Forms & Approvals.clue: Per 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A, this type of change to type design might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or airworthiness.
Forms & Approvals.explanation: A major alteration is a type design change that might appreciably affect structural strength, flight characteristics, or other airworthiness qualities — requiring approved data such as an STC or Form 337.
Forms & Approvals.learningObjective: State the definition of a major alteration per Part 43 Appendix A

Forms & Approvals.clue: An STC installation includes this document, which classifies the installation as a major alteration regardless of the part's TSO status.
Forms & Approvals.explanation: A flight manual supplement changes the aircraft's approved operating limitations or procedures — that change to type design qualifies the installation as a major alteration under Part 43 Appendix A, regardless of the part's TSO approval.
Forms & Approvals.learningObjective: Classify STC installations with flight manual supplements as major alterations

Forms & Approvals.clue: Replacing a TSO part with another TSO part of the same type, done per approved data, is generally not classified as this.
Forms & Approvals.explanation: TSO-for-TSO replacement of the same type per approved data is not a major alteration because it does not appreciably change the aircraft's airworthiness qualities. The two key qualifiers are 'same type' and 'per approved data.'
Forms & Approvals.learningObjective: Determine that TSO-for-TSO replacement per approved data is not a major alteration

Repair Station Ops.clue: When installing avionics wiring in a Part 145 shop, this advisory circular — or the aircraft manufacturer's wiring data — is the primary standard to follow.
Repair Station Ops.explanation: AC 43.13-1B Chapter 11 covers aircraft electrical wiring acceptable methods. When manufacturer wiring data exists it takes precedence, but AC 43.13-1B is the primary fallback. AC 20-165B covers ADS-B. AC 91-78 covers EFBs. AC 43.13-2B covers aircraft structures.
Repair Station Ops.learningObjective: State the primary wiring standard for avionics installation in Part 145

Repair Station Ops.clue: Under Part 145, these two roles share responsibility for ensuring current technical data is available and used during maintenance.
choices: The Quality Control department and accountable manager
The FAA FSDO and repair station owner
The technician and their direct supervisor
Repair Station Ops.explanation: Part 145 places responsibility for current technical data on the Quality Control system and the accountable manager. QC ensures data is current and available. The accountable manager is the person the FAA holds responsible for the station's overall compliance.
Repair Station Ops.learningObjective: State who is responsible for ensuring current technical data availability under Part 145

Repair Station Ops.clue: Most Part 145 repair station manuals require personnel to complete recurrent training at least this often.
Repair Station Ops.explanation: The industry standard in Part 145 repair station manuals is recurrent training every 24 months — aligning with other 24-month aviation maintenance cycles. The specific interval is set in each station's manual; 24 months is the standard most stations use.
Repair Station Ops.learningObjective: State the typical recurrent training interval in Part 145 station manuals

Repair Station Ops.clue: Before using specialized test equipment in a Part 145 shop, a technician must verify that this is current and within its interval.
Repair Station Ops.explanation: All precision test equipment must have a current calibration label. Using out-of-calibration equipment invalidates measurements and may produce legally non-compliant maintenance records.
Repair Station Ops.learningObjective: Verify test equipment calibration before use in a Part 145 shop

Repair Station Ops.clue: A Part 145 repair station's authorized scope of work is defined and limited by these, listed in its operations specifications.
choices: Its ratings and classes in the Ops Specs
Repair Station Ops.explanation: Operations specifications define what a Part 145 station is authorized to do. Individual A&P certificates authorize the individual technician, not the shop organization. ISO certification is a quality management standard, not an FAA authorization.
Repair Station Ops.learningObjective: Explain that Part 145 shop work is limited to its specific ratings and classes

Repair Station Ops.clue: Even if a radio is on the repair station's capability list, maintenance cannot legally proceed without this being available in the shop.
choices: The current revision of the manufacturer's maintenance manual
Repair Station Ops.explanation: Capability list listing provides authorization, but Part 145 also requires current technical data. An outdated manual does not satisfy this requirement — authorization and current data are both required, not interchangeable.
Repair Station Ops.learningObjective: Determine when maintenance cannot proceed due to missing current technical data

Inspections & Testing.clue: In general aviation aircraft, mono audio is primarily used for these two applications.
choices: Flight director cues and GPWS alerts
Inspections & Testing.explanation: GA audio systems use mono (single-channel) audio for intercom communications and speaker monitoring. Stereo separation provides no operational benefit for ATC communications or crew intercom — the same audio signal goes to all headsets and speakers.
Inspections & Testing.learningObjective: State the typical use of mono audio in GA aircraft

Inspections & Testing.clue: When troubleshooting an intermittent NAV flag on a VOR receiver in a Part 145 shop, this is the required first action before replacing any components.
choices: Swap the receiver with a known-good unit to isolate the LRU
Pull the circuit breaker and reset the system
Check the antenna coax cable for continuity
Inspections & Testing.explanation: Part 145 requires maintenance to be performed per approved technical data. Consulting the maintenance manual is the mandatory first step — it defines the approved diagnostic procedure. Swapping components without approved data is shotgun troubleshooting and a regulatory violation.
Inspections & Testing.learningObjective: Apply the correct first step when troubleshooting in a Part 145 shop

Inspections & Testing.clue: When inserting or extracting terminated wires from standard circular connectors, using these prevents pin damage and difficult-to-diagnose intermittent faults.
choices: Proper manufacturer-specified insertion and extraction tools
A heat gun and connector pick
Inspections & Testing.explanation: Manufacturer-specified insertion and extraction tools are required. Improvised tools damage pins, bend contacts, or crack the connector insert — creating intermittent faults that are extremely difficult to diagnose later.
Inspections & Testing.learningObjective: Apply correct insertion/extraction tool practice for circular connectors

Inspections & Testing.clue: Per 14 CFR 91.411, altimeter and pitot-static system inspections must be performed at this fixed interval — the same interval required for transponder checks under 91.413.
Inspections & Testing.explanation: Both 91.411 (altimeter/pitot-static) and 91.413 (transponder) require testing every 24 calendar months.

Inspections & Testing.clue: During a transponder test per Part 43 Appendix F, the test set verifies reply frequency, power output, suppression, and this parameter — which confirms the transponder correctly reports the aircraft's barometric altitude.
choices: Mode C altitude encoding accuracy within ±125 feet
ATC radar interrogation response delay within 3 microseconds
Squawk code assignment timing within 10 milliseconds
Inspections & Testing.explanation: Mode C altitude reporting accuracy must be verified within ±125 feet per Part 43 Appendix F.

Inspections & Testing.clue: An ELT inspection per 91.207 requires verifying battery expiration, proper mounting, antenna condition, and this — which must be performed only during the first five minutes of each hour to avoid triggering a false alert to search-and-rescue satellites.
choices: A full-duration 60-second transmission test on 406 MHz
A brief operational test limited to three audible sweeps on 121.5 MHz
A 30-second transmission test monitored on the aircraft's comm radio
No transmission test — only a visual inspection of the cockpit-mounted status light
Inspections & Testing.explanation: ELT operational tests are limited to three audible sweeps (approximately one second) and may only be performed during the first five minutes of each hour per 91.207(d).

Shop Safety & Hazmat.clue: In a Part 145 shop, cluttered walkways are dangerous primarily because of these three hazards.
choices: Fuel contamination, EMI, and oil spills
FOD, trip hazards, and tool loss
Shop Safety & Hazmat.explanation: Clutter creates FOD risk (debris entering aircraft), trip hazards for personnel, and tool accountability failures. These are the core reasons for mandatory housekeeping standards in Part 145 facilities.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.learningObjective: Explain why clean work areas are critical in a Part 145 shop

Shop Safety & Hazmat.clue: This class of fire involves energized electrical equipment and requires non-conductive extinguishing agents.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.explanation: Class C = energized electrical equipment. Class A = ordinary combustibles. Class B = flammable liquids and gases. Class D = combustible metals. Using water on a Class C fire creates an electrocution hazard.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.learningObjective: State that Class C fires involve energized electrical equipment

Shop Safety & Hazmat.clue: This document must be readily available for each hazardous material used in a Part 145 avionics shop.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.explanation: OSHA requires Safety Data Sheets (SDS) to be readily accessible for all hazardous materials in the workplace. In an avionics shop, this includes solvents, adhesives, coatings, and cleaning agents.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.learningObjective: State that an SDS must be available for each hazardous material in a Part 145 shop

Shop Safety & Hazmat.clue: To confirm a switch is causing a parasitic draw, you place this instrument in series with the circuit and activate the switch.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.explanation: Parasitic draw is current — measured with an ammeter in series. A voltmeter measures voltage in parallel. An ohmmeter measures resistance with power off. A wattmeter measures power but is not the standard tool for parasitic draw diagnosis.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.learningObjective: Confirm a parasitic draw source using ammeter in series

Shop Safety & Hazmat.clue: This is the primary reason hearing protection is critical in a busy avionics shop with engine run-up activity nearby.
choices: Short-term hearing loss affects radio communication quality during operations
OSHA requires it only for insurance liability compliance purposes
Sustained exposure causes cumulative permanent noise-induced hearing loss
Engine noise creates EMI that interferes with electronic test equipment
Shop Safety & Hazmat.explanation: Noise-induced hearing loss is cumulative and permanent — each unprotected exposure contributes to total damage even if no single event seems severe. It is a long-term occupational hazard, not just an acute discomfort issue.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.learningObjective: Explain why hearing protection is critical in a busy avionics shop

Shop Safety & Hazmat.clue: A pilot hears a constant tone on the frequency instead of ATC responses. This is the most likely diagnosis.
choices: ILS localizer signal interference on the comm frequency
Shop Safety & Hazmat.explanation: A constant tone replacing all voice traffic is the classic stuck PTT symptom. Someone's microphone is transmitting continuously and blocking the frequency. Squelch failure causes noise, not a tone. ATC transmitter failure causes silence. ILS bleed-over affects specific frequencies differently.
Shop Safety & Hazmat.learningObjective: Diagnose a stuck microphone as the cause of a constant tone on a comm frequency

final.clue: Under 14 CFR Part 43, this specific document must be completed and submitted to the FAA within 48 hours after a major repair or major alteration.
final.explanation: FAA Form 337 (Major Repair and Alteration) is required to document major repairs/alterations and must be forwarded to the FAA within 48 hours of returning the aircraft to service.

final.clue: A Part 145 Repair Station cannot perform alterations on an aircraft without approved data. Which of the following is NOT considered 'approved data' by the FAA?
final.explanation: AC 43.13-1B is 'acceptable data', not 'approved data' unless specifically approved by an FAA inspector for a major alteration. TCDS, ADs, and STCs are approved data.

final.clue: When approving an aircraft for return to service after a 100-hour inspection, the maintenance record entry must include this specific statement.
choices: I certify that this aircraft has been inspected in accordance with a 100-hour inspection and was determined to be in airworthy condition
Inspected and cleared per 14 CFR Part 91
final.explanation: 14 CFR 43.11 dictates the exact wording required for an inspection return to service: 'I certify that this aircraft has been inspected in accordance with... and was determined to be in airworthy condition.'
